content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Lars and the Real Girl

**Score:** 37
**Body:** “A delusional young man strikes up an unconventional relationship with a doll he finds on the Internet. In this comedy, Lars Lindstrom is an awkwardly shy young man in a small northern town who finally brings home the girl of his dreams to his brother and sister-in-law's home. The only problem is that she's not real - she's a sex doll Lars ordered off the Internet. But sex is not what Lars has in mind, but rather a deep, meaningful relationship. His sister-in-law is worried for him, his brother thinks he's nuts, but eventually the entire town goes along with his delusion in support of this sweet natured boy that they've always loved.” 

i was very caught off guard and moved and left emotional with this film! it does have it’s comedic moments, but overall so heartwarming and tear jerking.. just a good film to throw on when you don’t know what to watch.

 ryan gosling continues to grow on me as an actor. I also suggest his movie The Nice Guys!! 

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. easily could have gone sideways with the wrong director and lead actor. gosling really conveyed the pure but wounded soul trying to emotionally connect with someone he felt was real and the director really balanced the reality of the situation of mental illness/trauma and a man forming a bond with a sex doll with compassion and humour. 
2. You would probably enjoy "Harvey," with James Stewart.
  - i’m going to look this up on IMDB right now
    - Enjoy!
3. My friend suggested this to me, and it was not at all what I was expecting. I was expecting it to be more comedic, but it left me with a lot of thoughts and questions. Such as, would I go along with Lars and his dilution?
  - truly makes you wonder!!
4. LOVE this movie 
5. It’s a beautiful movie. I really think the screenplay should’ve won an Oscar but instead it went to Juno. I think Lars is far more clever and just better all around to be honest. It’s very original and different and quirky.

It’s a real gem of a movie about acceptance. I think it’s about time I rewatch this movie. 

Go Bianca!! 
6. I've had some challenges letting go from a past relationship and shared with my therapist that I feel like Lars and the Real Girl. It makes me sad when I think that. 
7. I love this soundtrack. Also The Nice Guys is a favorite of mine too!

## 2. Need movies that feel like Mulholland Drive, Donnie Darko, Twin Peaks...

**Score:** 26
**Body:** Hello guys! My favourite movies are Mulholland Drive, Donnie Darko, Fight Club and Twin Peaks (not a movie but i love the show).  
I'm really looking for that vibe. Don't know how to explain it exactly, but you know, those dark movies, setted in the 80s-90s kind of. The ones you end up sitting on the sofa, looking at the wall, reconsidering your life once you finish them. You probably know what feeling i'm talking about. So yea, I would really love to read your reccomendations based on the movies and the feeling / nostalgia i'm looking for.  
Peace!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Blue Velvet (1986)
  - I second this. More David Lynch is what you’re looking for. 
2. Under the Silver Lake
3. Cronenberg’s Crash. 
4. Dangerous Lives of Altar Boys

Welcome to the Dollhouse

United States of Leland 

Igby Goes Down 

(this was like my fav genre as a misanthropic teen)
5. After Hours 
6. - Something In The Dirt
- Coherence
- A Scanner Darkly
- Baby Driver
- Being John Malkovich
- Fear and Loathing in Las Vegas
- The Truman Show
- Little Miss Sunshine
- Castle Rock, if you feel like a tv show
7. A lot of Cronenburg, a lot of Peter Greenaway, and early Jean-Pierre Jeunet might interest you.

Various Korean films of the 2000's and 2010's.

Rashomon.

Brazil.

And of course the vast majority of David Lynch's output.

8. Enemy (2013)


9. Primer

Resolution

Upstream Color

## 3. Best Movies with stories/premises that you never heard before.

**Score:** 26
**Body:** Not just random crazy concept

I meant a story so original which feel so fresh. 

It could be of any genre

preferably grounded stories like Martin McDonough movies because sci-fi or fantasy genre makes it easier to make something new. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Truman Show (it's really no movie like it) 

Coherence has a really interesting thought provoking stuff

Tennet might do too much at times but it's really interesting 


  - I like the Truman Show + Matrix as examples of "how do you know what's real?"  One is everyone is in on it but you, and the other is everyone is being duped.  Throw in Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind or Memento for you "you are duping yourself" .
2. Contact 

Sew-Torn

Pulp Fiction


3. Clerks was that, at the time.

So was Thelma & Louise.
4. Being John Malkovich

Donnie Darko

Her (Well, at the time at least)

Eraserhead

Swiss Army Man

The Lobster

A Different Man 
5. Arrival
6. The Game

The Matrix

Memento

The Usual Suspects

Breakdown

A Few Good Men

Seven

Point Break

Forrest Gump
7. Weekend at Bernie's, son.
8. Man from earth 
9. Denis Villeneuve's *Arrival*, *Sicario*, or even *Prisoners*.

## 4. Best Alien Invasion Movies? Or Solid Alien Movies Non Comedy ?

**Score:** 21
**Body:** just looking for some good alien movies to make me feel on the lookout or that make you question things.. 

I do love THE FOURTH KIND <3

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Arrival (the best imo)

Signs

Close Encounters Of The Third Kind
2. Fire in the Sky (1993)
3. Invasion of the Body Snatchers, both the one from the ‘50s and the ‘70s remake, they’re both great.

Captive State was an alien invasion movie I really liked, although it flew under the radar – I’ve never met anyone else who saw it!

Cloverfield too, although it almost crosses the line into horror.
4. Arrival is a masterpiece
5. District 9
6. The Abyss (1989) and Dark Skies (2013).

This is exactly what you need trust me 🍻🤘
7. No One Will Save You
8. Enemy Mine.
9. Battle of Los Angeles 
10. The Vast of Night

## 5. Looking for a movie that the main character dies and keeps reviving that shows the agony of dying thousands of times

**Score:** 19
**Body:** The closest I could find is Edge of Tomorrow, But it's too plot-heavy instead of focusing more on the psychological aspects, which is what I wanted. 

I'm requesting cuz I want to write a script myself about this topic :)

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Mickey 17 (2025) it isn't thousands of times but it highlights it pretty well
2. Happy Death Day
3. The Prestige 
4. Russian Doll (streaming series)
5. Boss level was a fun movie.
6. Groundhogs day
A map of tiny perfect things
Palm springs
Edge of tomorrow
The endless
  - Oohh The Endless.  That one guy in the tent
7. The Old Guard (2020). We don't see thousands of deaths, but it is implied that the main characters have died at least several hundreds of times.
8. Palm Springs does a really good job of this. 
9. it’s not a movie, but the first season of russian doll.

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. This is one of the most brutal scenes I have seen in any movie or series. The way McConaughey delivered it with a stoic face made it even more extraordinary. (True Detective Season 1) What’s your favorite dialogue from the character Rust Cohle?

**Score:** 4159

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. "I got a bad taste in my mouth. Aluminum. Ash. Like I can smell the psychosphere."

"I got an idea. Let's make the car a place of silent reflection from now on."
  - "You wanted me to open u-"

"Yeah, and now I am begging you to shut the fuck up."
    - This is even funnier when you listen to Woody talking about how much he hated McConaughey‘s method acting. 
      - McConaughey being method to the point he actually helped his partner transpire disdain for his character by being insufferable IRL. That's professionalism right there.
  - An underrated part of that scene is that Marty doesn’t know what psychosphere means and replies by calling it a “psycho’s fear” 
2. Because it’s Thursday and it’s past noon. Thursday is one of my days off. On my off days I start drinking at noon. You don’t get to interrupt that.
3. "If the only thing keeping a person decent is the expectation of divine reward, then brother, that person is a piece of shit."
4. The way they ended the series was brilliant imo and capped off one of the most impressive seasons of any TV show ever.

"Marty, about the sky thing. You're looking at it wrong."

"How so?"

"Once there was only dark. You ask me, the light's winning."
  - It’s the greatest TV (Mini)Show ever
5. Whenever my wife asks how I slept I answer "I don't sleep anymore.  I just dream."  She just rolls her eyes at me.

## 2. Heat (1995). This was SO GOOD. An actual 10/10

**Score:** 1790
**Body:** I think this is the best LA has ever looked in a movie

10/10 cinematography

9/10 music

8/10 acting

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. ![gif](giphy|xOD2M0aHX5hjIbUbq9)

And you’ve got your head all the way up it!
  - bGREATASS
2. For me, the action *is* the juice. 
  - Those rifle shots alone deserve an imax remaster. 
3. Just because no one has brought it up yet, this frame in the film is a homage to the painting Pacific, by Alex Colville: [https://www.aci-iac.ca/art-books/alex-colville/key-works/pacific/](https://www.aci-iac.ca/art-books/alex-colville/key-works/pacific/)
4. The diner scene is chef’s kiss
  - I had coffee with McCauley HALF AN HOUR AGO! 😡
5. https://preview.redd.it/kf9rx01ym4vg1.jpeg?width=447&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=f94e429a85926320f517950e16025c6794314df7

I love how beautiful and stylistic this film is visually. Along with a solid plot and immersive sound design.
6. Stunning filmmaking. Been on my rewatch list for a wile, your post has moved it up the list, thank you!
7. Loved Voight in it

## 3. Sin City is 21 years old and is still the greatest comic book adapted movie ever made... imo

**Score:** 1436

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. 21 years - whaaaaat
  - ![gif](giphy|l0IyajjbNiRvCr7RC)
2. Between this and 300, Frank Miller's work was strongly adapted to the screen.
  - Yeah, shot for shot.
  - Even the animated Dark Knight Returns films are among the strongest animated Batman films & my personal favorites 
3. Probably my favourite cinema experience. Such a great movie. A real shame Sin City 2 was so bad 
  - Have you watched the extras for Sin City?  

*Spoilers!*

Originally apparently when Bruce Willis’ character was being hung and the camera faded to black, the credits actually began to roll.  Then after a little bit of credits, the camera flashed back to Bruce Willis’ face as he shouted “NO!” Then rescued himself.  Apparently it was extremely poorly received by test audiences so they cut that idea.  But man, I wish they kept that version!
4. Sin City is great, no doubt. Watchmen, however, exists. 
  - While I won’t argue either of those opinions, I will triple down with V For Vendetta. It is eternal at this point. 
5. Blade

## 4. Gary Sinise, known for his role as Lieutenant Dan in Forrest Gump, has raised over $400 million to support wounded veterans.

**Score:** 1359

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. He's also a redditor! We love you, Gary. 
  - Lt dan got new legs…he can type with ty LT DAN we salute you.  Enjoy pinky’s on Fridays.  Cast of Apollo 13 go for fridays for hot dogs i heard!
2. Whilst also losing his son to a terrible cancer, and watching his wife go through and beat cancer
3. I've always loved that he's done that.. but what sucks about it is he shouldn't have to.
4. Before anyone decides to chime in on Sinise's politics, while he is a conservative, he is very outspokenly anti-Trump/MAGA.
  - Yes. Hes a REAL convervative ... not these crazy MAGA peeps. I respect that, seems like someone that can have an educated and respectful debate.
5. is nobody gonna talk about the 2nd picture?
  - Seems like a poor gift choice from Gary
6. As a Veteran, thanks, Gary! 
7. I met him over 20 years ago when he was a narrator for the Epcot Candlelight Processional. He took the time to meet a bunch of us high schoolers and shake hands with almost everyone. And his reading of the story of Jesus was masterful, it gave me goosebumps. 

Such a nice guy, so genuine. 

## 5. Robert Patrick was a great villain

**Score:** 940

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. That’s the dad on Bridge to Terabithia
  - And Peacemaker.
2. He was so underrated in *The X-Files*, the man can do evil robot or grieving father, big thumbs up from me.
3. and beware -- That nutjob b\*\*\*\* who killed this unquestionable hero is still on the loose!
4. In my opinion, his work on the Sopranos was his best work. 
5. And he informed us all that Wolfie is just fine, otherwise we would have never known.
6. Get back in your hole davey!
7. Peacemakers Dad, a real charmer.
8. https://i.redd.it/ybs6irhid6vg1.gif
9. Dude liked to admire nice bikes.

Also liked to network with the community to find a troubled young kid.

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Project Hail Mary (2026): I enjoyed this movie! However, I would have enjoyed it more if it wasn't chock full of cutesy cutesy humor [ spoilers]

**Score:** 215
**Body:** **Spoilers for this movie**

I feel like superhero cutesy humor dropped into the middle of very high stakes drama is sort of infecting the whole movie landscape now. Case in point... project Hail Mary. 

This is a perfectly enjoyable flick! I sat through it and thoroughly had a great time. However the constant and never ending cutesy humor really took me out of the moment multiple times it honestly got very annoying. For example when grace was telling rocky about the fact that his girlfriend now lived with somebody else named Dave and rocky responds with “I hate Dave”. And of course it's supposed to be very funny and I'm sure most people did think it's funny, but to me it's just sort of eye rolling. 

For one thing rocky didn't have the personality of an alien. He had the personality of a human being. 100% very human. He looked like an alien but his psychology was totally human. And all of the “fist my bump” stuff too. I guess it's amusing but it just felt like this movie was constantly trying to shoe horn in jokey jokey laugh a minute type situations that were very jarring with its actual subject matter of the literal destruction of the earth itself.

 A little bit of humor is definitely great to crack up a drama. But when it's just never ending constant little quips and jokes to me it gets old, especially when the nature of the jokes are very self aware and and honestly way too cute.

If the credits rolled on this movie and it said “directed by James Gunn” I wouldn't have been surprised. Because that's the type of humor we have here. I just wish it would've been more in the vein of “arrival” and less in the vein of guardians of the Galaxy

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The humor in the movie was actually toned *down* from the novel. Andy Wier writes one character well: middle-aged sarcastic White dude. It's clearly an idealized version of himself. So, any character he wants to be likeable - in PHM basically Grace and Rocky - he writes that way.
  - I haven’t seen the movie yet but I read the book a long while ago back before everyone was going crazy for it. I was not particularly blown away by it. I enjoyed it enough but am not a big fan of Andy Wier’s writing voice. I was pretty shocked to learn how universally loved it is when I found it “just ok”.
    - Weir writes novels with the hopes they'll be picked up as a TV show or movie. It's popcorn fun. 
    - It’s because Andy Weir is a few steps from an airport novel writer. That’s not a dig, just reality.
  - Yeah PHM did something that that I often see in Sorkin's work and makes me crazy: every single character has the example quippy sense of humor. Even the security guards and buttoned-down scientists 
  - Then he tries to put that middle aged white male character into the body of 20 something Saudi girl in Artemis and it falls really flat
2. Being more like Arrival would not have really worked, because the movie is fundamentally about Grace’s (physical and emotional) isolation and how his relationship with Rocky turns all that around. He goes from a guy who was forced on a suicide mission that would save his own planet to a guy who sacrifices his own life (intends to, at least) to save Rocky and Rocky’s planet. To that end, it’s crucial to the story that Rocky be a character that *we* as the audience also love and believe is worth dying for. 

The humor is a quick way to get to that, and is definitely a bit too easy/lazy as times. But I also think a dreadfully serious movie wouldn’t work as well for this type of story. And Rocky is adorable so it’s all good
3. I enjoyed the movie a lot as well, but your point really hits home during the scene where he is told he has to go on the mission. They were basically assaulting and drugging him against his will, and they made it seem like he was being a panicky, immature idiot. I didn't read the book, but was told by many people who did, that it was handled much more seriously in the book.
4. I got to say I disagree, but kind of to my own great surprise.


Meaning in general my likes and grievances seem very alligned with your own, I absolutely loathe this kind of James Gunn shit, and "cutesy" would be my own perfect word to describe it. My own favourite style would also be exactly that Villeneuve and Alex Garland vibe of sci fi, and that cutesy James Gunn and Marvel style invading that seems something I should feel really defensive about. 


But for some reason to me this movie pulled off the fusion tremendously!


Or put another way: Its imho exactly not some James Gunn influence, but more a completely different brand of cutesy, a tonal fusion in line with the great masters like Steven Spielberg or these all beloved Robin Williams classics. 


I would maybe encourage you to question your own assesment on your own terms, and see that you are just employing too impossible a standard. By your own words:


>This is a perfectly enjoyable flick! I sat through it and thoroughly had a great time.


The thing is, I wouldnt even disagree on exactly those couple groany moments that went too far for me too!


I would just say that by focussing on the couple misses, you are actually missing that the whole strength and magic of the movie is exactly pulling of this very impossible tonal fusion.


If you liked the movie, you might actually also focus on just about every scene you liked about the movie and notice how those all seem dangerously cutesy and close to groan worthy too!


E.g the first time he meets rocky and they are doing this little mirror dance and then he does a couple goofy poses and those get mirrored too, and then they both kind of chuckle... This could also be seen as dangerously cutesy and groan worthy, but imho thats just a fantastic and magical scene!


Same with just about every other scene! Walk through the movie scene by scene and thats just about 70% of the movie, its all like that. Look at it through that lense and you just have to admit that there is no taking the cutesy out of the movie, its just at the very heart of what it is.


I think especially focussing on the fantastic soundtrack shows very much how extremely self-aware the director was that he is walking this extremely hard to pull off high wire walk tonal contradiction, and being extremely artistic about it, the way the soundtrack hits this fusion of the fantastic-grandiose and the whimsical. I was very impressed by just that aspect, looked up some interview with the composer, and they worked on the soundtrack extremely hard over 1-2 years, and it doesnt surprise me in the slightest.


I think all in all, if you generally liked the movie you just have to admit that you are more complaining about it having some failures on its very own terms: It doesnt hit the tonality as pitch perfect as you want it to, and thats where you have these groany moments.



I think with a movie like that you really have to pay proper respect and acknowledge that even in those groany scenes like the "fist my bump" the director is very much self-aware and ahead of you and you got to imagine him sitting there torturing himself if he wants to keep that in there, or if it goes too far tonally! You got to admit that with guys on that level they might be fully aware that it will be some groan moment that people will complain or meme about, but still judge it as necessary for the overall movie! Play it safe, like amateurs like you and me, cut every "too groany" or "too cheesy" moment and in the end you will have some flat thing sitting at 6,6 on imdb.


Its this kind of very hard to pull off tonal balance, but thats exactly why its those kinds of movies that become those all beloved evergreens. I would make a bet its propably already up there in the pantheon with flicks like the Shawshank Redemption or Good Will Hunting, etc..
5. I agree. Entertaining but not a top tier science fiction film. Grade of B for me. Nice to see smart people doing smart things w/o a villain hanging over everything. Too flippant at times which took away from the gravity of the situation. Rocky became too anthropomorphized for my taste.  The science could have been better explained. I enjoyed it for the most part.

## 2. We Need To Talk About Ben Wheatley

**Score:** 64
**Body:** I continue to be baffled by Ben Wheatley’s career as a director, which in some ways mirrors Sam Raimi’s early-to-mid career in reverse. It is not quite as jarring as David Gordon Green’s path from indie darling, with *George Washington* and *All the Real Girls*, to director-for-hire in the late-aughts Rogen/Goldberg/Franco weed-comedy ecosystem (*Pineapple Express*, *The Sitter*, *Your Highness*). Still, Wheatley remains difficult to pin down, and his choices often feel less eclectic than confusing. For those unfamiliar with his work, his films tend to begin as genre exercises and then lurch, often thrillingly, into abrupt and shocking violence.


Like most directors, Wheatley began in advertising, moved into television, and then made the leap into features with 2009’s *Down Terrace*, a kitchen-sink crime drama about a family watching its tiny empire collapse from within. The film announced a director who could work comfortably inside genre while quietly undermining its conventions. Rather than settling into one mode, Wheatley kept veering sideways. *Kill List*, his second film, is typically described as a psychological horror crime film. His third, *Sightseers*, is a pitch-black romantic comedy about a strange middle-aged couple who fall in love while spree-killing their way across England. Think *Natural Born Killers*, but dumpier and with more caravans.


That restlessness initially felt like a strength. Wheatley seemed to be doing what ambitious young directors are supposed to do, choosing projects that tested his range without sanding off his instincts. *A Field in England*, written by his wife and creative partner Amy Jump, is a black-and-white psychological horror film set during the English Civil War, but it retains the cramped, intimate feeling of his earlier work. It also points toward *High-Rise* and *Free Fire*, films built around enclosed spaces where tension, absurdity, and violence are trapped together until they combust.


That period now looks more coherent in retrospect, and not just because the films themselves are so distinctive. *Sightseers* was Wheatley’s first film that he did not write, and he has written only half of his twelve features. Many of the others were written by, or co-credited to, Jump. Wheatley has suggested their partnership was never a neat division of labor, with him directing and Jump writing and editing while he handled the public-facing side of the work and she remained largely out of view. Whatever the exact balance, his post-Jump career increasingly suggests that the collaboration was not easily separable.


*Free Fire* was the last film they made together, though they briefly worked on a *Tomb Raider* project before leaving during pre-production, after which Jump’s script was rewritten. There may still have been some residual energy from that partnership in *Happy New Year, Colin Burstead*, the 2018 film Wheatley wrote and directed, a loose reworking of *Coriolanus* that returned him to the quieter social realism of his earlier work. Seen now, it feels a little like a final stable point before the career took a stranger turn.


I remember having a similar reaction when Sam Raimi, after climbing from B-movie wunderkind to Hollywood A-lister through a run of films that culminated, critically at least, in *A Simple Plan*, followed it with the Kevin Costner baseball drama *For Love of the Game*. At the time, as a grad student in film, it felt like a betrayal. It felt worse when Raimi spoke in interviews about wanting to grow up and make films he could take his wife to. To me, it looked suspiciously like a director trying to pivot toward prestige after the critical and financial success of *A Simple Plan*. Maybe that is too harsh, but it is hard not to think of that when considering Wheatley’s decision to direct Netflix’s 2020 adaptation of Daphne du Maurier’s *Rebecca*.


It was an odd fit. The problem was not simply that Hitchcock’s 1940 version was beloved, canonical, and the only Hitchcock film to win Best Picture. It was that Wheatley never seemed like a director who would thrive under the weight of tasteful literary inheritance. The reviews mostly circled the same question: why? Some critics noted that you could almost feel the film straining between two competing impulses. On one side was the source material, stately and overdetermined. On the other was Wheatley’s attraction to something stranger, uglier, and more feverish. You could sense him wanting to slip the leash, but *Rebecca* never lets him.


Then came *In the Earth*, which felt, briefly, like a recalibration. Wheatley wrote and directed it, and it returned him to familiar ground: natural horror, hallucinatory dread, and the kind of loopy, unstable weirdness he does better than almost anyone. It was well received, and for a moment it looked as though he had found his footing again.

And then came *Meg 2: The Trench*.


I have always loved the possibly apocryphal John Sayles story, the one where he writes bad movies so he can afford to direct good ones. The legend is that he used his paycheck from writing *Piranha* to help fund *The Return of the Secaucus 7*. That is the frame I tried to apply to Wheatley directing a Jason Statham giant-shark sequel. The first *Meg* had been critically underwhelming but commercially successful, and maybe this was simply a work-for-hire payday. Fair enough. Everyone deserves one.


The problem is that *Meg 2* barely feels like a Ben Wheatley film at all. Whether that comes down to studio interference, a dead-on-arrival script, or simple misalignment, the result is inert. There are faint glimpses, especially late in the film, of what a truly campy and unhinged Wheatley take on the material might have looked like. But those moments vanish almost as soon as they arrive. Mostly, the film is flat, clumsy, and strangely lifeless. It is hard to reconcile it with the same filmmaker who made a black-and-white horror film set in the English countryside feel electric and genuinely uncanny. That same year, Wheatley and David Gordon Green were both nominated for Worst Director at the Razzies, eventually losing to Rhys Frake-Waterfield for *Winnie-the-Pooh: Blood and Honey*, which won in basically every category it touched. Not great company.


Since then, Wheatley has followed his shark detour with *Generation Z*, a one-season British television series, and more recently *Bulk*, which has been described as a return to form, full of dark humor, practical effects, and formal experimentation. I have not seen *Bulk*, so I am willing to believe the reports. In fact, I would like to.


Which brings us to the present, and to *Normal*, the 2026 actioner that feels less like a comeback than another step sideways into irrelevance. Without saying too much about the film itself, it is difficult to imagine it wowing critics or lingering in the culture, especially as a Ben Wheatley film. More than anything, the Bob Odenkirk vehicle feels like a mishmash of *Fargo* aesthetics and the increasingly worn-out *Nobody* formula. At times it even resembles the kind of movie Odenkirk and David Cross would have savaged on *Mr. Show*.


There are still traces of Wheatley in it. He has always had a knack for filming violence so that the camera seems to move as an extension of impact itself. Fists lunge, bodies snap, eyeballs are skewered, and the hyperviolence is often comically abrupt. But here those flourishes feel detached from anything underneath them. *Down Terrace* showed that Wheatley cared about character, or at least understood that violence only really lands when we care who it is happening to. In *Normal*, everyone feels interchangeable. Good or bad, likable or unlikable, no one seems to exist for any reason beyond surviving long enough to reach the final scene. Even Odenkirk looks as though he knows it, offering little more than a blood-smeared smirk and a shrug.

So yes, someone should probably check in on Ben Wheatley. Is he okay? Is he still happy making films? Amy Jump is, in my imagination, off doing exactly the kind of thing people mean when they say, “Fuck Hollywood and this filmmaking bullshit, I’m going to read and do some gardening,” and honestly, good for her. Maybe Wheatley should join her for a while.

Because the frustrating thing is that the talent is still plainly there. At his best, Wheatley has a real gift for turning genre material inside out, for locating the absurd, violent, destabilizing edge in stories that might otherwise play as exercises. It would be great to see him and Jump work together again, because their collaborations, while imperfect, were often beautiful. Failing that, he still seems capable of becoming something like a Verhoeven or a Sirk, a filmmaker who can smuggle a sly, subversive intelligence into commercial genre work. But that would require the right material, the right freedom, and maybe a renewed interest in the thing that made his early films feel alive in the first place.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Not much to add but I appreciate your write up and agree with pretty much all of it. I remember when Kill List came out there was a palpable buzz and excitement around him and that movie even with all its flaws delivers on making you feel extremely queasy in a unique way that’s not easy to come by. But the subsequent movies for me left me cold, even tho I actually think his High Rise adaptation is pretty underrated and does the novel as much just as you can with the limited budget and scope. 
2. Reading this is giving me whiplash, I had no idea how active a filmmaker Wheatley was. A Field in England is a film that I love returning to and In the Earth didn't cut me as deep at first but still kept coming up in my thoughts for years. 3. He's one of my favourite directors, and I find his career fascinating.

The DVD extras on A Field In England are effectively a masterclass in how to make a film on zero budget, and it becomes very clear when watching them that Wheatley just absolutely loves the medium. To me his career is that of someone who just wants to make films, in whatever genre or style that might be, and to see what he can do with the budget he's given.

The Meg 2 feels to me like Wheatley being given the opportunity to make a big budget action blockbuster and him saying, "fuck it, why not". It's not very good and it's not a genre he's particularly suited to, because it's not a genre that's fundamentally about weird little guys doing weird little things, which I think is what's at the heart of all of his best work.

I haven't seen Normal yet but I'm cautiously optimistic. Odenkirk has shown he can do that sort of role in Nobody and it's another film about a weird guy doing weird guy shit. I'm hoping it will hit a similar tone to Free Fire, which I love, but I guess I'll wait and see.

4. He’s basically a DIY filmmaker who occasionally gets given a decent budget. I asked him once at a Q&A what the difference is between making a Hollywood film and making a film like Colin Burstead, and he said the only difference is a lot more crew to work in a lot more roles*.

I think he’s a “make money with blockbusters to fund my mad experiments, and try to have fun doing it”. He seems to be much more into making his weird British films like Into the Earth or his recent one with Sam Riley, but I think he gets a kick out of e.g. getting to make a big shoot ‘em up with Bob Odenkirk. He’s a film fan who occasionally gets to cone to Hollywood and play in a bigger sandpit.

*He was really funny and down to Earth and seems to have no pretensions whatsoever.

Free Fire and High Rise weren't perfect but thoroughly enjoyable, while I really thought his early films captured so much of what Britain is to me. I still think they are some of the best films to come out of Britain in the last 25 years.

I'm not going to criticise the guy for making massive movies like the meg 2. I'm sure his pay check is worth it, and it doesn't lower the quality of his earlier work for me.

6. Ben Wheatley is the most frustrating filmmaker I've ever experienced. He has a great visual eye, his films have interesting premises, but he can't tell a story for shit. Some people view the nonlinearity and gaps in logic in his films as intentional misdirection or him wanting to challenge the audience. I see it as a director who doesn't know how to direct. Him and his wife know how to start a story but they have no idea how to end them, and they have nothing to say. Might've been one of my favorite directors in a parallel universe.

I'm a huge JG Ballard fan, and I rather like Wheatley's version of High Rise because I literally don't understand it, and that makes me think about the material much more deeply than I would if I simply ingested it like any other movie. It also looks exactly like I would have wished it to: slick, trashy, cold, and desolate.

At this point, I don't chase after his work because I never have the satisfying feeling of being on the same page with Wheatley. Kill List was probably the closest for me, and after reading about his inspiration and intentions, I felt like I hadn't gotten it because I'm American, and that's OK. Everything isn't for me. Maybe Wheatley isn't for me.
8. A fascinating read. *A Field in England* is there with the best. Such a rare treat to get a historical drama that is about the occult and it's shot in black and white. I'm still in disbelief that this guy then directed *Meg 2*.

## 3. Is Black Hawk Down (2001) an epic war movie or a deliberately misframed depiction of events?

**Score:** 48
**Body:** Everything falls apart fast with bad intel, no clear plan, and total chaos, but the movie frames it in a way that makes it feel huge and heroic anyway.

The focus isn’t on the mistakes but on how intense it all feels. Honestly, I never bought into that. Looking back, it feels like a much needed post-9/11 morale booster.

For the younger heads in here, check out A Bridge Too Far (1977).

It lays out a failed mission without trying to dress it up. No sugarcoated propaganda, just a plan that didn't work and the tragic cost of it.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. On a related note, check out the 2022 documentary Theaters of War, which explores the “military-entertainment complex”. The military provides cheap/free access to equipment to Hollywood, and in return Hollywood makes movies that portray the military in a positive light. It analyses several war movies, including Black Hawk Down.
  - I havent seen the documentary but does it talk about Cher's modelling a skimpy bikini all over the deck of a NAVY ship on the 'If I Could Turn Back Time' video? Federal armed forces took action immediately after seeing the video.
    - Yvan eht nioj?
2. >Everything falls apart fast with bad intel, no clear plan, and total chaos, but the movie frames it in a way that makes it feel huge and heroic anyway.

Black Hawk Down is a complicated film. The situation in Somalia must be understood in the context of its two most notable operations.

Operation Restore Hope (Help feed the starving people and stabilize the country by ensuring the safety of aid convoys through the country).

Operation Gothic Serpent (Apprehend tyrannical warlord Mohamed Farrah Aidid).

The reason this is complex is that America entered Somalia with the best of intentions, acting as part of a UN aid mission to help an impoverished nation in peril. But they became embroiled in a civil war, where they did not fully understand or appreciate the political landscape of Somalia.

Black Hawk Down is a film about people who are trying to help a country that is torn between wanting help (because starvation isn't fun) and having profound dysfunctional problems that the Americans have no idea how to fix. Black Hawk Down is a tragedy of heroism gone wrong. The great liberators have an ideological mission. A mission that many in Somalia didn't understand. They believed that the Americans were just imperialist oppressors.

Americans had a naive understanding of societies like Somali. Somalia was clan-based. A never-ending series of clan-based power grabs, hoarding all the resources for your clan. It's not that the clans sought the extermination of other clans, but rather that they sought the supremacy of their own clan.

So the Americans stumble into this with a mindset of good guys, bad guys, order vs chaos. They thought that their Somalian allies and the Somalian people wanted to be free in the same way that Americans understand freedom. They were like, okay so who do we put into power that will ensure the food gets to everyone and there's government and the buses run on time. But that wasn't something Somalia had the building blocks for. They were a failed state. A collapsed dictatorship dominated by clan conflicts.

Black Hawk Down uses its aesthetics and its US military perspective to focus on the failed mission, but the real question the viewer is meant to ask themselves is whether it was worth it.

The US stood by and let the Rwandan Genocide happen a mere six months later in part because of "Mogadishu Syndrome." The paralysis triggered by the Operation Gothic Serpent Fiasco. You get involved in a military conflict, maybe another Black Hawk crashes on live television, you discover the people you were supporting are also guilty of a huge number of war crimes. Do you occupy Rwanda indefinitely? The Clinton Administration hugely regretted not intervening after the fact, but this paralysis of "We tried to help, and it blew up in our faces" is why the US pivoted so hard away from idealistic humanitarian efforts towards a cynical "what's in it for us?" attitude. Because if they were gonna have dead American soldiers on TV it would play better if they were defending American interest and not dying in a chaotic tribal massacre. (Which the US tried to spin as a normal civil war.)

This is why Black Hawk Down is a very different kind of film to A Bridge Too Far. Because it implicitly touches on the nature of war when the war doesn't involve you directly. When you are acting as judge, jury, executioner for other people under the cloak of US exceptionalism and noble ideas.
  - I feel like you described the book but not the film lol. 
  - What you just described is what the film could have been, and most likely would have been a better film. Instead we got what the OP is critiquing. The film is quite formulaic and provides the viewer with all the stereotypes we’ve been grown accustomed to since the 80’s. It was engaging and Scott adeptly presents the action in a visceral way.
3. Black Hawk Down isn’t about the failed mission and US interference in a foreign war. It’s about the brotherhood of the SOCOM soldiers. 2 snipers giving their lives to save a helicopter pilot. Soldiers running into a hornets nest to bring back their fellow soldiers and survivors guilt. 

Plus it came out in 2001, it was filmed before 9/11. So it can’t be a post attack “morale booster”. 
  - That's all it is. 
4. The depiction of Somali fighters definitely leans towards propaganda. They’re generally shown to be ill-trained, undisciplined, and only kill Americans through luck and numbers. In reality, the SNA soldiers had significant combat experience and would have acted like it, and the numerical advantage wasn’t as great as Scott made it seem. Depicting the enemy as brutish hordes who win unfairly is a common propaganda tool that was used by the Germans against the Soviets and the Japanese against us. You can also see it in more recent movies like Lone Survivor, which inflated the count of Afghan militia (from roughly 10 to nearly 100) to save the SEALs the embarrassment of losing to an inferior force.

## 5. The Labor Economics of 'Alien' - and its lessons for inequality on Earth

**Score:** 31
**Body:** [This article from Planet Money](https://www.npr.org/sections/planet-money/2026/04/14/g-s1-117075/the-labor-economics-of-alien-and-its-lessons-for-inequality-on-earth?utm_source=facebook.com&utm_term=nprnews&utm_medium=social&utm_campaign=npr) dives into the economic background of 'Alien' from a labor force perspective. I do not know what the overall economy and labor market were like in the 70s (film released in 1979) but in today's tight labor market it seems like an oddly prescient warning of unchecked corporate power (Skydance-Paramount +/- Warner Bros, Amazon-MGM; obviously more in other industries, see Kraft-Heinz Co). 

>From Alien's Wikipedia:   
  
The company that owns the *Nostromo* is not named in the film, and is referred to by the characters as "the company". However, the name and logo of the company appears on several set pieces and props such as computer monitors and beer cans as "Weylan-Yutani".[^(\[85\])](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alien_(film)#cite_note-McIntee,_15-86) Cobb created the name to imply a business alliance between Britain and Japan, deriving "Weylan" from the [British Leyland Motor Corporation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/British_Leyland_Motor_Corporation) and "Yutani" from the name of his Japanese neighbor.[^(\[86\])](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alien_(film)#cite_note-McIntee,_28-87)[^(\[87\])](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alien_(film)#cite_note-88) The 1986 sequel, [*Aliens*](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aliens_(film))*,* named the company "Weyland-Yutani",[^(\[86\])](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alien_(film)#cite_note-McIntee,_28-87)[^(\[88\])](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alien_(film)#cite_note-aliens-89) and it has remained a central aspect of the franchise.



>Excerpt from the article:   
  
The Weyland-Yutani Corporation is a terrible employer. Take the original *Alien* movie. Ripley (played by Sigourney Weaver) and the rest of her crew on the USCSS Nostromo spaceship are essentially space truckers, hauling mineral ore across the galaxy for the company. The distance is so far they have to go into cryogenic sleep for the journey. But, on their way home to Earth, the company reroutes them. The ship's computer awakens the crew after picking up a mysterious signal on the moon of a distant planet, and the company puts in motion a plot to get them to go to it.  
  
The workers complain. One says he wants to go home, and they deserve a bonus if they have to do work beyond what they signed up for. But another crewmember — who is secretly an android doing the company's bidding — tells them that their contract's fine print says any signal like this must be investigated, or else their contract is void and they have to forfeit their salaries. The company has all the leverage. With no bargaining power, the workers comply without getting extra pay for overtime work.

I thought it was an interesting take on one of my favorite movies (my other favorite takes being - this is why scientists stick to protocol; and this is what happens when men don't listen to women). Enjoy!

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Let’s not forget Alien’s world building shares roots with Scott’s 1982 Blade Runner, in Philip K Dick’s Do Androids Dream of Electric Sheep? That book epitomizes Dick’s vision of a totally commoditized Earth: polluted to the point of uninhabitability. Those who are not wealthy enough or skilled enough to emigrate Offworld are left behind. Offworld colonies, on the other hand, are supported by resources that must be extracted from other worlds so inhospitable that labor there is performed by Androids - artificial humans who have sentience but not autonomy. Those humans who “make it” Offworld do so by the grace of the Company and serve at their pleasure or get sent back to Earth. 

Dick had a very clear vision of the labor economics in play, and Scott put some work into building them into the Alien and Blade Runner franchises.
2. >when men don't listen to women

Dallas isn't ignoring Ripley.

He's following the orders of a computer that calls itself *Mother*. Dallas discounts Ripley because he is following the chain of command - which is another incisive critique of capitalism. Everyone in the film who numbly does their job as ordered dies. Ripley lives because she acts for herself.
